turns.content: ugh ok I need to vent about a coworker situation before I lose my mind

so there's this nurse on my unit, Brittany, who transferred to nights about two months ago from days. and ever since she got here she's been acting like nights is some kind of punishment and she's too good for it and it's driving everyone on our team crazy

Oh, the "I'm only here temporarily" attitude from a new night shifter. That's rough because it basically signals to everyone who chose to be there that their shift is lesser. What specifically is she doing that's causing problems?

like she'll say things about how on days they did things differently (better, obviously) or she'll complain about how quiet it is and how she's bored, and meanwhile the rest of us are like... we love nights?? I genuinely love working nights, it's calmer, there's less management breathing down your neck, and the night team is so tight because you really depend on each other when there's less staff. she just doesn't get it

That's a real cultural clash. Night shift teams tend to develop a strong bond precisely because of what you described — smaller crew, more autonomy, less hierarchy, and a shared experience that day shift people don't understand. When someone comes in and treats it like exile, it's not just annoying, it's disrespectful to the culture you've all built.

Is she actually bad at the job, or is it purely the attitude? Because those are two different problems.

she's competent, I'll give her that. she's not unsafe or anything. it's the attitude and honestly how it's affecting team morale. like last week she was complaining in the break room about missing "real" nursing because nights is so slow and my friend Jasmine who's been on nights for 6 years just got up and left the room because she was so pissed. nights isn't slow, it's different, and we still have codes and emergencies and admissions, she acts like we just sit around

The "real nursing" comment would make anyone's blood boil. That's essentially saying the work your whole team does isn't legitimate.

Jasmine walking out was probably the right move in the moment — better than saying something she'd regret. But the fact that it's affecting people who've been there for years means this isn't a minor annoyance anymore.

A few questions:
- Does Brittany report to the same charge nurse or manager as the rest of you?
- Has anyone directly addressed it with her, or is it all behind-her-back frustration right now?
- Is she actively trying to transfer back to days, or is she stuck on nights for some reason?

same charge nurse yeah. and nobody has said anything directly to her which is part of the problem I think. we all just vent to each other about it. and I don't know if she's trying to go back to days, I think she might have been moved because of some staffing issue but I'm not sure

So right now you have a situation where everyone is frustrated, nobody's addressed it, and it's festering. That's going to get worse, not better, because Brittany probably has no idea how much damage she's doing. She might even think she's just making conversation when she compares days vs nights.

You have a few options, roughly in order of escalation:

**1. Direct conversation (peer to peer):**
Someone she respects — or at least someone willing — pulls her aside privately. Not aggressive, just honest: "Hey, when you say things about nights being slow or not real nursing, it lands badly with the team. Most of us chose this shift and we take pride in what we do here. I know you didn't mean it that way but I wanted you to know."

This is the fastest fix if she's a reasonable person who's just oblivious.

**2. Go through the charge nurse:**
If nobody wants to do the direct conversation, or if it's tried and doesn't work, your charge nurse needs to know this is affecting team morale. Frame it as a team dynamics issue, not a personal complaint about Brittany.

**3. Integrate her:**
Sometimes the "days was better" attitude comes from feeling like an outsider. If she hasn't been welcomed into the night shift culture, she might be clinging to her day shift identity because she doesn't feel like she belongs yet. This doesn't excuse the comments, but it might explain them.

Which of these feels most realistic for your situation?

honestly I think option 1 is probably right but nobody wants to be that person including me. but also I kind of feel like it should be me because I've been on nights for 4 years and I actually like it here and I can explain why without being hostile about it. Jasmine would just go off on her lol

The fact that you genuinely love nights is actually your biggest advantage in this conversation. You're not coming from a defensive place of "don't insult my shift" — you can come from a place of "let me tell you why this shift is actually great."

If you want to do it, here's a framework:

**Setting:** Catch her during a quiet moment, not in front of everyone. Break room when it's just the two of you, or even walking to the parking garage after shift.

**Tone:** Warm, not confrontational. You're inviting her in, not calling her out.

**Something like:**
"Hey Brittany, I know the transition to nights is an adjustment. I wanted to be honest with you about something — when you compare days to nights or talk about it being slow, it kind of stings for the team because most of us specifically chose to be here. I've been on nights for four years and I love it. The autonomy, how close the team is, the pace. It's different from days but it's not less. I think once you settle in you might actually like it too, but the comparisons are making it harder for people to warm up to you."

**Then give her an opening:** "Is there something about the transition that's been hard? Because we've all been through it."

This way you're being honest but also extending a hand. If she responds well, you might actually help her adjust. If she doesn't, you escalate to the charge nurse knowing you tried.

ok that's actually really good and not as scary as I was making it in my head. I think the key is doing it when it's just us because if other people are around it'll feel like an ambush. maybe after shift tomorrow when we're walking out

After shift walking out is perfect — it's natural, low-pressure, and there's a built-in exit for both of you (you get to your cars and it's over). Nobody's trapped in a room.

A couple things to keep in mind:
- Don't over-rehearse it. You know what you want to say. If you script it too much it'll sound weird.
- If she gets defensive, don't escalate. Just say "I'm not trying to make it a thing, I just wanted you to know how it's landing" and let it sit. Sometimes people need a day to process before they can hear it.
- After the conversation, don't immediately report back to the team about what happened. That turns it into gossip and undermines the trust you're trying to build with her.

You sound like someone who handles things with empathy, so I think this will go fine. And even if it's awkward, the team will benefit from someone finally saying something.

the don't report back to the team thing is a good point because I know Jasmine is going to ask me about it immediately lol. ok I feel better about this. honestly the night crew is like my second family and I just want to protect that vibe you know? we work hard and we look out for each other and I don't want one person to mess that up

That protectiveness over your team says a lot about the culture you've all built. A tight night shift crew is genuinely one of the best things in healthcare — and you're right to want to protect it.

Just tell Jasmine "I talked to her, give it some time" and leave it there. If Brittany changes her behavior, the team will notice on their own.

Good luck tomorrow morning. You've got this.
